“Hello,” answered a bright voice inside.

story:
Mr Riley began most mornings at the town library, a short walk from his bungalow at the top of the main street in a Suffolk market town where he and his wife had retired. He’d once thrived in the library’s writing group—part workshop, part group therapy—but the old crowd had drifted away or died, and only he remained, along with Mrs Peterson, the head librarian nearing retirement. She had known his late wife, Estella, and liked to exchange a few words with him, not every day, but most days. He was a fixture in his corner, reading the paper. Afterwards, he crossed to the Hideout café for coffee. It was only a little life, but a life all the same.

At home, a cheerful “Hello” greeted him from the hall. The voice belonged to his African grey parrot, relocated from the lounge because it interrupted his television. It had been Estella’s idea, and now, with Estella gone, he was left with the bird’s uncanny echoes: “Hello.” “Would you like a cuppa tea?” It coughed when he coughed; it peppered his house with phrases. He fed it seeds, resisted an unkind word, and thought about the small job he had planned for the afternoon. He’d heard scratching in the attic the night before—mice, he guessed, moving in from the garden to make themselves comfortable in the eaves for winter.

He changed into overalls, fixed a dust mask, and took down the rod to release the attic hatch. As he passed, the parrot chirped, “That’s the ticket.” He climbed the ladder with traps and a margarine tub of peanut butter—he’d read mice preferred it to cheese—and edged into the hot, dusty space where the rafters met the ceiling joists. Sweat prickled his back in the rockwool. He pushed traps into the dim eaves. Then the heart attack struck. His chest clamped as if seized by a great claw. He lay back, breath scraping, and called out, “Help me.”

“What’s the time?” the parrot called up.

He drifted in a fevered in-between of heat and cold, waking and sleep, rationing strength to call again for help.

Mrs Peterson walked passed Mr Riley’s house on her way home from the library, and as she hadn’t seen him for two days, she decided to call in to see if he was alright. She walked up the path and knocked on the door. “Hello,” she called.

“Hello,” answered a bright voice inside.

“Are you alright up in the rockwool, Mr Riley?” she asked. A cough sounded from the hall.

“Help me,” came faintly from the attic, too thin for her to hear.

“What’s the time?” the parrot asked.

“About half past five,” she answered, indulgent. “Do you need anything? I’m on my way home.”

“Would you like a cuppa tea?” the parrot offered.

“No thanks, George will be expecting me.”

“That’s the ticket,” the parrot said.

She walked on. Two days later she tried again. By then Mr Riley was dead, rigid in the baked attic. She knocked. “Hello.”

“Hello,” the voice said. “Just the ticket.”

“You sound better,” she said, relieved, and went home.

Two more days passed. The parrot stood on its perch. Beside it, the feeder had just emptied. It lifted one foot, cocked its head, scratched—then called out, loud and clear into the quiet bungalow: “Help me.”
